summary: No-ship: the HITL slice has material approval-integrity gaps. `/resume` is authorizing by `runId` only, the CSRF/origin check accepts any localhost origin, and the approved-command shape for shell-string steps is so coarse that one harmless approval unlocks arbitrary later shell scripts.

findings.title: `/resume` binds approval only to `runId`, so a stale or duplicated resume can approve a later, different pause in the same run
findings.recommendation: Key pending resumes by a per-pause nonce/version, include that nonce in the emitted pause event, and require `POST /resume` to present the matching nonce (and ideally expected pause kind/stepIndex). Reject resumes for any superseded pause.

findings.title: The Origin guard accepts any `localhost`/loopback origin, so another local web app can cross-origin POST `/resume` and approve commands
findings.recommendation: Treat origin as full origin, not just hostname. Allow only the exact viewer origin(s) or require an unguessable CSRF token tied to the pause/run. As written, `http://localhost:any-port` passes the guard, and `access-control-allow-origin: *` makes scripted abuse straightforward.

findings.title: Approving one shell-string command approves all future shell scripts because every `{ command: "..." }` collapses to `bash -c <SCRIPT>`
findings.recommendation: Do not use a single opaque shape for shell strings. Either forbid `command` strings in consented steps, require argv-only commands, or include a meaningful normalized script fingerprint/tokenized script structure so consent remains specific to the actual command being approved.
